question: What property of carbon mainly explains why it forms a very large number of compounds?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the key reason carbon forms so many compounds.
explanation.principle: Carbon has four valence electrons, so it can form four covalent bonds. It also links to itself strongly, a property called catenation.
steps: Carbon can bond with four atoms at the same time.
Carbon–carbon bonds are strong, so long chains and rings can form.
Because of these two features, millions of stable organic compounds are possible.
This is why carbon chemistry is far richer than the chemistry of most other elements.
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon is special because it can make four bonds and can keep joining to other carbon atoms to make chains and rings. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In methane, CH₄, carbon is said to be tetravalent because it

options.D: shares four pairs of electrons with itself

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret what tetravalency means using methane.
explanation.principle: Valency is the combining power of an atom. Tetravalency means valency 4.
steps: In CH₄, one carbon atom is bonded to four hydrogen atoms.
Each C—H bond is a covalent bond formed by sharing electrons.
Since carbon makes four bonds here, it shows valency 4.
So carbon is described as tetravalent.
explanation.simple_explanation: Tetravalent simply means 'able to form four bonds'. In methane, carbon does exactly that. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which statement best explains catenation in carbon compounds?

options.A: carbon readily loses all its valence electrons
options.B: carbon atoms form strong bonds with one another
options.C: carbon has the highest atomic number among non-metals
options.D: carbon exists only as giant ionic crystals

answer_text: carbon atoms form strong bonds with one another

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the idea of catenation.
explanation.principle: Catenation means self-linking of atoms of the same element.
steps: Carbon atoms can bond to one another strongly.
These strong C—C bonds allow chains, branched chains and rings.
That is why compounds like propane, hexane and benzene are possible.
Therefore catenation is due to strong carbon–carbon bonding.
explanation.final_answer: B. carbon atoms form strong bonds with one another
explanation.simple_explanation: Catenation means an element joins to itself. Carbon does this very well because C—C bonds are strong and stable. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which structure correctly illustrates the tetravalency of carbon in ethane?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the structure that shows each carbon making four bonds in ethane.
explanation.principle: Ethane has molecular formula C₂H₆ and contains a single bond between the two carbon atoms.
steps: In H₃C—CH₃, each carbon forms three bonds with hydrogen and one bond with carbon.
That gives each carbon a total of four covalent bonds.
Option B is ethene, option C is ethyne, and option D is an ether, not ethane.
So the correct structure for ethane is H₃C—CH₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: In ethane, each carbon must have four bonds altogether: three to hydrogen and one to the other carbon. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Why are most carbon compounds covalent rather than ionic?

options.A: carbon usually gains four electrons completely
options.B: carbon usually shares electrons to complete its octet
options.D: carbon has no electrons in its outer shell

answer_text: carbon usually shares electrons to complete its octet

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why carbon usually forms covalent compounds.
explanation.principle: Carbon has four electrons in its outer shell.
steps: If it tried to lose four electrons or gain four electrons completely, that would require too much energy.
Instead, carbon usually attains a stable arrangement by sharing electrons.
That sharing leads to covalent bonds, as seen in methane, ethene and benzene.
Hence most carbon compounds are covalent.
explanation.final_answer: B. carbon usually shares electrons to complete its octet
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon usually completes its outer shell by sharing electrons, so most of its compounds are covalent. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The general formula of alkanes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the general formula for alkanes.
explanation.principle: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons containing only single C—C bonds.
steps: For an open-chain alkane with n carbon atoms, the number of hydrogen atoms is 2n + 2.
Examples are methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆ and propane C₃H₈.
These all fit CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
So the general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons, and their open-chain members follow the formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The compound CH₃CH₂CH₃ is correctly named

explanation.question_restate: We are to give the IUPAC name of CH₃CH₂CH₃.
explanation.principle: The structure has three carbon atoms joined by single bonds only.
steps: The prefix for three carbons is prop-.
The suffix for single bonds only is -ane.
Therefore the compound is propane.
It is not propene or propyne because there is no multiple bond.
explanation.simple_explanation: Three carbons give 'prop-' and all single bonds give '-ane', so the name is propane. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name for CH₃CH=CH₂?

explanation.question_restate: We are to name an unsaturated hydrocarbon.
explanation.principle: The molecule has three carbon atoms, so the prefix is prop-.
steps: It contains one carbon–carbon double bond.
The suffix for one double bond is -ene.
Hence CH₃CH=CH₂ is propene.
It is not propane because it is unsaturated.
explanation.simple_explanation: A double bond changes the ending from -ane to -ene. With three carbons, the name becomes propene. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The IUPAC name of HC≡C—CH₃ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to name a three-carbon hydrocarbon containing a triple bond.
explanation.principle: The structure has three carbon atoms, so the prefix is prop-.
steps: A carbon–carbon triple bond gives the suffix -yne.
Thus HC≡C—CH₃ is propyne.
It is an alkyne, not an alkene or alkane.
So the correct answer is propyne.
explanation.simple_explanation: Three carbons give 'prop-' and a triple bond gives '-yne', so the correct name is propyne. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which member of the homologous series has the molecular formula C₄H₁₀?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the compound from its molecular formula.
explanation.principle: For alkanes, the general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
steps: Therefore C₄H₁₀ is an alkane with four carbon atoms: butane.
The corresponding alkene would be C₄H₈.
Hence butane is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Using the alkane formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, C₄H₁₀ matches butane. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The empirical formula of a compound containing 40% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen and 53.3% oxygen is

explanation.question_restate: We are to find the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms from percentage composition.
explanation.principle: Empirical formula means the simplest mole ratio of the atoms present.
steps: Assume 100 g of the compound: C = 40 g, H = 6.7 g, O = 53.3 g.
Divide by the smallest value, about 3.33, to get 1 : 2 : 1.
Therefore the empirical formula is CH₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: Empirical formula means the simplest ratio. After converting mass to moles and simplifying, the ratio is 1:2:1, giving CH₂O. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A hydrocarbon contains 85.7% carbon and 14.3% hydrogen by mass. Its empirical formula is

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine the simplest formula from percentage composition.
explanation.principle: The method is to assume 100 g, convert masses to moles, then simplify the ratio.
steps: Assume 100 g: carbon = 85.7 g and hydrogen = 14.3 g.
Moles of carbon = 85.7/12 ≈ 7.14; moles of hydrogen = 14.3/1 = 14.3.
Divide both by 7.14 to get approximately 1 : 2.
So the empirical formula is CH₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: After turning the percentages into moles, the simplest ratio is 1 carbon to 2 hydrogens. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A compound has molecular formula C₆H₁₂O₆. What is its empirical formula?

explanation.question_restate: We are to reduce the molecular formula to the simplest whole-number ratio.
explanation.principle: The empirical formula is obtained by dividing all subscripts by their highest common factor.
steps: In C₆H₁₂O₆, the subscripts are 6, 12 and 6.
The highest common factor is 6.
Dividing by 6 gives C₁H₂O₁.
So the empirical formula is CH₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: The empirical formula is just the simplest version of the molecular formula. Divide all subscripts by 6. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: If the empirical formula of a compound is CH₂ and its relative molecular mass is 56, the molecular formula is

explanation.question_restate: We are to move from empirical formula to molecular formula.
explanation.principle: The molecular formula is a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula.
steps: Empirical formula mass of CH₂ = 12 + 2 = 14.
Number of empirical units = 56/14 = 4.
Multiply the empirical formula by 4.
(CH₂)₄ = C₄H₈.
explanation.simple_explanation: First find how many times the empirical formula fits into the molar mass. Then multiply the subscripts by that number. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: An organic compound contains carbon, hydrogen and oxygen only. If 0.1 mol of the compound has a mass of 6.0 g and the empirical formula is CH₂O, what is its molecular formula?

explanation.question_restate: We are to use both the empirical formula and molar mass.
explanation.principle: Molar mass is obtained from the mass of a known amount in moles.
steps: If 0.1 mol has a mass of 6.0 g, then 1 mol has a mass of 60 g.
Empirical formula mass of CH₂O = 12 + 2 + 16 = 30.
Number of empirical formula units = 60/30 = 2.
Therefore the molecular formula is (CH₂O)₂ = C₂H₄O₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Find the molar mass first, then compare it with the empirical formula mass. A factor of 2 gives C₂H₄O₂. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes and homologous series
question: Members of a homologous series have the same

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a defining feature of a homologous series.
explanation.principle: A homologous series is a family of organic compounds with the same functional group and general formula.
steps: Successive members differ by a –CH₂– unit.
They show similar chemical properties because the functional group is the same.
Their physical properties change gradually, not remain exactly the same.
So the correct description is same functional group and general formula.
explanation.final_answer: B. functional group and general formula
explanation.simple_explanation: A homologous series is like a family: same functional group, same general formula, and each new member differs by CH₂. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes and homologous series
question: Which of the following pairs are consecutive members of the alkane series?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify consecutive compounds in the alkane family.
explanation.principle: Consecutive members of a homologous series differ by one –CH₂– unit.
steps: Propane is C₃H₈ and butane is C₄H₁₀.
Their formulas differ by CH₂.
The other pairs are not both alkanes.
Therefore propane and butane are consecutive alkane members.
explanation.simple_explanation: Consecutive alkane members must both be alkanes and differ by CH₂. Propane and butane satisfy this. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes and homologous series
question: The main reaction characteristic of alkanes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common reaction type of alkanes.
explanation.principle: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons because they contain only single bonds.
steps: Since they do not have multiple bonds, they do not readily undergo addition like alkenes.
Their typical reaction is substitution, especially with halogens in sunlight.
For example: CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl.
Hence substitution is the characteristic reaction.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alkanes are saturated, so they usually react by replacing one atom with another, especially in halogenation. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes and homologous series
question: Which statement about the boiling points of alkanes is correct?

options.A: they decrease as molecular mass increases
options.C: they generally increase as molecular mass increases
options.D: they depend only on the number of double bonds

answer_text: they generally increase as molecular mass increases

explanation.question_restate: We are to relate boiling point to size in the alkane series.
explanation.principle: As alkane molecules become larger, intermolecular forces become stronger.
steps: More energy is then needed to separate the molecules.
So methane has a lower boiling point than pentane, and pentane lower than decane.
Thus boiling points generally increase with molecular mass.
This is one of the gradual changes seen in a homologous series.
explanation.final_answer: C. they generally increase as molecular mass increases
explanation.simple_explanation: Bigger alkane molecules attract one another more strongly, so they boil at higher temperatures. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes and homologous series
question: Complete combustion of an alkane produces mainly

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the products of complete combustion.
explanation.principle: Complete combustion means burning in sufficient oxygen.
steps: Carbon in the fuel is fully oxidized to CO₂.
Hydrogen in the fuel is oxidized to H₂O.
So the main products are carbon dioxide and water.
Incomplete combustion would give carbon monoxide or soot.
explanation.simple_explanation: When an alkane burns completely in enough oxygen, carbon becomes CO₂ and hydrogen becomes H₂O. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkenes, addition and polymerization
question: The general formula of alkenes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the general formula for open-chain alkenes.
explanation.principle: Alkenes contain one carbon–carbon double bond.
steps: For one double bond in an open chain, the formula is CₙH₂ₙ.
Examples are ethene C₂H₄ and propene C₃H₆.
These fit CₙH₂ₙ.
Therefore the correct formula is CₙH₂ₙ.
explanation.simple_explanation: Open-chain alkenes have one double bond and follow the formula CₙH₂ₙ. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkenes, addition and polymerization
question: Which reagent is commonly used to test for unsaturation in an organic compound?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common test for unsaturation.
explanation.principle: Alkenes and alkynes are unsaturated because they contain multiple bonds.
steps: Bromine water is decolourized by unsaturated compounds through addition across the multiple bond.
A saturated alkane does not rapidly decolourize bromine water under ordinary conditions.
Therefore bromine water is the standard test here.
It is a favorite exam application of addition reactions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Unsaturated compounds add bromine across their multiple bonds, so bromine water loses its colour. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkenes, addition and polymerization
question: Ethene reacts with hydrogen in the presence of nickel to form

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the product of hydrogenation of ethene.
explanation.principle: Hydrogenation means addition of hydrogen across a multiple bond.
steps: Ethene, CH₂=CH₂, contains a double bond.
Hydrogen adds across that double bond in the presence of a catalyst such as nickel.
The double bond becomes a single bond.
The product is CH₃—CH₃, which is ethane.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogenation means adding H₂ across a double bond. Ethene therefore becomes ethane. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkenes, addition and polymerization
question: Poly(ethene) is produced from ethene by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the type of polymerization undergone by ethene.
explanation.principle: Ethene molecules contain carbon–carbon double bonds.
steps: During polymerization, the double bonds open up and many monomer molecules join together.
No small molecule like water or HCl is eliminated.
That process is called addition polymerization.
Hence poly(ethene) is an addition polymer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Addition polymers form when unsaturated monomers like ethene join together without eliminating any small molecule. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkenes, addition and polymerization
question: Which statement best explains why alkenes are more reactive than alkanes?

options.B: they contain a reactive π bond in the double bond

answer_text: they contain a reactive π bond in the double bond

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the reactivity of alkenes and alkanes.
explanation.principle: A C=C double bond contains one sigma (σ) bond and one pi (π) bond.
steps: The π bond is weaker and more exposed than the σ bond.
Because of this, reagents can attack the double bond more easily.
That is why alkenes undergo addition reactions readily.
So the reactive π bond explains their greater reactivity.
explanation.final_answer: B. they contain a reactive π bond in the double bond
explanation.simple_explanation: The extra π bond in alkenes is easier to break during reactions, so alkenes are usually more reactive. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The general formula of alkynes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the general formula for open-chain alkynes.
explanation.principle: Alkynes contain one carbon–carbon triple bond.
steps: For one triple bond in an open chain, the formula is CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
For example, ethyne is C₂H₂ and propyne is C₃H₄.
These fit CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
Therefore CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Open-chain alkynes contain a triple bond and follow the formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The simplest member of the alkyne series is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the first possible alkyne.
explanation.principle: A triple bond needs at least two carbon atoms.
steps: So a one-carbon alkyne cannot exist.
The first member is C₂H₂, called ethyne.
Its common name is acetylene.
Therefore ethyne is the simplest alkyne.
explanation.simple_explanation: You need at least two carbons to form a triple bond, so the first alkyne is ethyne. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following compounds can decolourize bromine water due to a triple bond?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the unsaturated compound that contains a triple bond.
explanation.principle: A triple bond occurs in alkynes.
steps: C₂H₂ is ethyne, which contains a carbon–carbon triple bond.
Unsaturated compounds decolourize bromine water by addition.
C₂H₄ also decolourizes bromine water but due to a double bond, not a triple bond.
Therefore C₂H₂ best fits the question.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ethyne has a triple bond, so it is unsaturated and reacts with bromine water. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: When excess hydrogen is added to ethyne in the presence of a catalyst, the final product is

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the product of complete hydrogenation of ethyne.
explanation.principle: Complete hydrogenation means enough hydrogen is supplied to remove all unsaturation.
steps: Ethyne has formula HC≡CH.
One molecule of H₂ can reduce it to ethene.
With excess hydrogen, a second addition occurs.
The final saturated product is ethane, CH₃—CH₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Adding enough hydrogen to a triple bond eventually converts it fully into a single bond, giving an alkane. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Compared with ethene, ethyne contains

options.A: more hydrogen atoms per carbon atom
options.B: the same number of σ bonds between the carbon atoms
options.C: one less bond between the carbon atoms
options.D: no shared electrons between the carbon atoms

answer_text: the same number of σ bonds between the carbon atoms

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare bonding in a double bond and a triple bond.
explanation.principle: A double bond consists of one σ bond and one π bond.
steps: A triple bond consists of one σ bond and two π bonds.
So both ethene and ethyne have one σ bond between the carbon atoms.
What differs is the number of π bonds.
Hence they have the same number of σ bonds between the carbon atoms.
explanation.final_answer: B. the same number of σ bonds between the carbon atoms
explanation.simple_explanation: Both double and triple bonds contain one σ bond. The triple bond simply has one extra π bond compared with the double bond. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Structural isomers are compounds that have the same

options.A: empirical formula but different molecular masses
options.B: molecular formula but different structural formulae
options.C: general formula but different functional groups only

answer_text: molecular formula but different structural formulae

explanation.question_restate: We are to define structural isomerism.
explanation.principle: Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different atom-to-atom arrangements.
steps: However, the atoms are connected in different ways.
That different connectivity gives different structural formulae.
Hence they may have different properties even though their molecular formula is the same.
So the correct definition is same molecular formula, different structural formulae.
explanation.final_answer: B. molecular formula but different structural formulae
explanation.simple_explanation: Structural isomers contain the same numbers of atoms, but those atoms are arranged differently. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following is a structural isomer of butane?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify another compound with formula C₄H₁₀.
explanation.principle: Butane has molecular formula C₄H₁₀.
steps: 2-methylpropane also has formula C₄H₁₀ but a different arrangement of atoms.
That makes it a structural isomer of butane.
The others do not all have the same formula as butane.
Therefore 2-methylpropane is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Both butane and 2-methylpropane are C₄H₁₀, but the carbon skeleton is arranged differently. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Geometric isomerism is possible in some alkenes because of the

options.A: free rotation around the double bond
options.B: restricted rotation around the double bond

answer_text: restricted rotation around the double bond

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why cis-trans isomerism occurs.
explanation.principle: A C=C double bond prevents free rotation of the bonded atoms.
steps: If each carbon in the double bond is attached to two different groups, two arrangements become possible.
These are geometric isomers, often called cis and trans.
So restricted rotation about the double bond is the key condition.
This is why alkanes usually do not show such isomerism around C—C single bonds.
explanation.final_answer: B. restricted rotation around the double bond
explanation.simple_explanation: Because the double bond cannot rotate freely, atoms can stay locked into different spatial arrangements such as cis and trans. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following can show geometric isomerism?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an alkene capable of cis-trans isomerism.
explanation.principle: For geometric isomerism, each carbon of the double bond must have two different groups attached.
steps: In CH₃CH=CHCH₃ (but-2-ene), each double-bond carbon has H and CH₃ attached.
So cis- and trans- forms are possible.
Ethene does not qualify because each carbon has two identical H atoms.
Therefore but-2-ene can show geometric isomerism.
explanation.simple_explanation: But-2-ene can exist as cis and trans forms because each carbon of the double bond is attached to two different groups. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: n-Butane and 2-methylpropane differ in

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the basis of isomerism between two alkanes.
explanation.principle: Both compounds have formula C₄H₁₀ and both are alkanes.
steps: What differs is the way the carbon atoms are connected.
n-Butane has a straight chain while 2-methylpropane is branched.
Therefore they differ in carbon skeleton arrangement.
This is a common kind of structural isomerism called chain isomerism.
explanation.final_answer: C. arrangement of the carbon skeleton
explanation.simple_explanation: They are chain isomers: same formula, same class, but different carbon branching. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Crude oil is best described as

options.A: a pure compound of carbon and hydrogen
options.C: a compound made only of methane
options.D: a mixture of salts dissolved in water

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe crude petroleum correctly.
explanation.principle: Crude oil is not a single substance.
steps: It contains many hydrocarbons of different chain lengths and boiling ranges.
It also contains small amounts of sulfur, nitrogen and other impurities.
So crude oil is a mixture of many hydrocarbons.
That is why it must be refined before use.
explanation.final_answer: B. a mixture of many hydrocarbons
explanation.simple_explanation: Petroleum is not one chemical; it is a complex mixture of many hydrocarbon compounds. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Fractional distillation separates crude oil mainly because the components have different

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the principle behind fractional distillation.
explanation.principle: In a fractionating column, different components vaporize and condense at different temperatures.
steps: This happens because they have different boiling points.
Lower-boiling fractions rise higher before condensing, while higher-boiling fractions condense lower down.
Therefore separation depends mainly on boiling point differences.
This is why the temperature gradient in the column is important.
explanation.simple_explanation: Each petroleum fraction boils over a different temperature range, so they can be separated in a fractionating column. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which petroleum fraction is obtained near the top of the fractionating column?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify which fraction is collected near the coolest part of the column.
explanation.principle: The top of the column is cooler.
steps: The fractions with the lowest boiling points rise highest before condensing.
Refinery gases have the lowest boiling points among the major fractions.
So they are collected near the top.
Heavier fractions condense lower down.
explanation.simple_explanation: The top of the column is coolest, so the lightest, lowest-boiling fractions such as refinery gases are found there. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following is a major use of bitumen?

explanation.question_restate: We are to match a petroleum fraction with its common use.
explanation.principle: Bitumen is a very heavy, viscous residue from crude oil.
steps: Because it is sticky and waterproof, it is widely used in road surfacing and roofing.
It is not a light fuel fraction like gasoline or kerosene.
Hence its major use is road surfacing.
This is a standard applied chemistry fact in exams.
explanation.simple_explanation: Bitumen is the heavy sticky residue used especially for road construction. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In a fractionating column, the temperature is

options.A: highest at the top and lowest at the bottom
options.C: lowest at the top and highest at the bottom

answer_text: lowest at the top and highest at the bottom

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe the temperature gradient in a fractionating column.
explanation.principle: Crude oil enters near the bottom after heating.
steps: The bottom remains hottest to keep heavier fractions vaporized or partially vaporized.
As vapours rise, the column becomes cooler.
Hence the top is the coolest region and the bottom is the hottest.
This gradient is what makes separation possible.
explanation.final_answer: C. lowest at the top and highest at the bottom
explanation.simple_explanation: A fractionating column is hot at the bottom and cooler at the top, so different fractions condense at different heights. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Cracking, reforming, petrochemicals, octane number and vulcanization
question: Cracking is important in the petroleum industry because it

options.A: converts small molecules into larger ones only
options.B: converts heavy fractions into more useful smaller molecules

answer_text: converts heavy fractions into more useful smaller molecules

question: The octane number of petrol is a measure of its ability to

question: Vulcanization improves natural rubber by

question: Benzene has the molecular formula

question: Which diagram best represents the modern structure of benzene?

answer_text: a six-membered ring with a circle inside

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the best modern representation of benzene.
explanation.principle: Benzene has delocalized π electrons spread round a six-membered ring.
steps: Older drawings show alternating single and double bonds.
However, the electrons are actually delocalized around the ring, making all six C—C bonds equal in length.
So the best modern symbol is a hexagon with a circle inside.

A simple classroom sketch is:
  ______
 /      \
|   ◯    |
 \______/

explanation.final_answer: B. a six-membered ring with a circle inside

question: Compared with alkenes, benzene is less likely to undergo addition because it

options.B: is aromatic and stabilized by delocalized electrons

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the unusual stability of benzene.
explanation.principle: Benzene has a ring of delocalized π electrons.

Addition reactions would destroy that stable aromatic system.

question: In the Lucas test, tertiary alcohols react faster than primary alcohols because tertiary alcohols

question: Glycerol is best described as

explanation.simple_explanation: In ketones, the carbonyl group is inside the chain, so the carbonyl carbon is attached to two carbon atoms. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

explanation.final_answer: B. reduce the blue solution to a brick-red precipitate
explanation.simple_explanation: Aldehydes reduce Fehling's solution and produce a brick-red precipitate, while ketones usually do not. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Soaps clean mainly because they

question: Detergents are often preferred to soaps in hard water because detergents

question: Cellulose differs from starch mainly because cellulose

So amino acids are correct.
