explanation.simple_explanation: Ninhydrin is associated with amino acids because it reacts with their amino groups. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Millon's reagent is especially used to test for proteins containing the amino acid

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall what makes Millon's test positive.
explanation.principle: Millon's reagent responds to phenolic groups in proteins.
steps: The amino acid tyrosine contains a phenolic ring and gives the characteristic test.
Therefore proteins containing tyrosine respond to Millon's reagent.
So the best answer is tyrosine-like phenolic group.
This is a more advanced but exam-relevant protein test.
explanation.simple_explanation: Millon's test is linked with the phenolic group in tyrosine residues found in some proteins. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The primary structure of a protein refers to the

options.B: sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain

answer_text: sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain

explanation.question_restate: We are to define primary protein structure.
explanation.principle: Protein structure can be described at different levels.
steps: The primary structure is the exact order in which amino acids are arranged along the chain.
This sequence determines later folding and function.
Therefore it refers to the amino acid sequence.
It does not mean the final three-dimensional shape.
explanation.final_answer: B. sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain
explanation.simple_explanation: Primary structure simply means the order of amino acids from one end of the chain to the other. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Enzymes are generally

options.A: carbohydrates that speed up reactions permanently by being used up
options.B: proteins that act as biological catalysts

answer_text: proteins that act as biological catalysts

explanation.question_restate: We are to define enzymes.
explanation.principle: Enzymes are biological catalysts made mainly of proteins.
steps: They speed up chemical reactions in living organisms.
They do this without being permanently used up.
So the best definition is proteins that act as biological catalysts.
This links proteins to many body functions.
explanation.final_answer: B. proteins that act as biological catalysts
explanation.simple_explanation: Enzymes help reactions happen faster in living systems and are usually proteins. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: One function of the enzyme amylase is to catalyse the breakdown of

options.C: fats to fatty acids only in soap making

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the action of amylase.
explanation.principle: Amylase is a digestive enzyme.
steps: It catalyses the hydrolysis of starch.
The starch is broken down into simpler sugars such as maltose and eventually glucose.
Therefore amylase acts on starch.
This is an important biological function.
explanation.simple_explanation: Amylase digests starch, turning it into smaller sugar molecules. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Enzymes are effective catalysts because they

options.B: provide an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
options.D: work best at any temperature without limit

answer_text: provide an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how enzymes speed up reactions.
explanation.principle: Every reaction has an energy barrier called activation energy.
steps: Enzymes lower this barrier by providing a better reaction pathway.
As a result, more reacting particles can react successfully at the same temperature.
So enzymes increase reaction rate by lowering activation energy.
This is why small amounts of enzyme can have large effects.
explanation.final_answer: B. provide an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Enzymes speed reactions up by making it easier for reactants to react. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which statement about enzymes is correct?

options.A: they are destroyed in every reaction they catalyse
options.B: they are highly specific in their action
options.C: they work equally well in all pH values

answer_text: they are highly specific in their action

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a key property of enzymes.
explanation.principle: An enzyme has an active site whose shape suits particular substrate molecules.
steps: That is why one enzyme usually catalyses one type of reaction or a small group of closely related reactions.
This is called specificity.
Therefore enzymes are highly specific in their action.
They are not destroyed in the reaction they catalyse.
explanation.final_answer: B. they are highly specific in their action
explanation.simple_explanation: Enzymes are selective: each one usually fits and acts on particular substrates. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: At very high temperatures many enzymes stop working mainly because

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why high temperature can stop enzyme action.
explanation.principle: Enzymes are proteins with specific three-dimensional shapes.
steps: Excess heat can disrupt the bonds that maintain this shape.
When the active site changes shape, the substrate may no longer fit.
This loss of proper shape is called denaturation.
Therefore enzyme action can stop at very high temperatures.
explanation.simple_explanation: Too much heat can damage the shape of an enzyme, especially its active site, so it stops working properly. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A polymer is a substance formed by

options.A: breaking a large molecule into smaller units
options.B: joining many small molecules called monomers
options.D: neutralizing an acid with a base

answer_text: joining many small molecules called monomers

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a polymer.
explanation.principle: A polymer is a very large molecule made by repeating many smaller units.
steps: The smaller units are called monomers.
These monomers join together in a polymerization process.
So a polymer is formed by joining many monomers.
This is the basis of plastics, fibres and rubbers.
explanation.final_answer: B. joining many small molecules called monomers
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of a polymer as a long chain made by linking many small repeating units together. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Nylon is produced mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the type of polymerization used for nylon.
explanation.principle: Nylon is formed from monomers that contain functional groups able to react together.
steps: During the process, small molecules such as water may be eliminated.
This type of reaction is called condensation polymerization.
Therefore nylon is a condensation polymer.
This distinguishes it from poly(ethene).
explanation.simple_explanation: Nylon forms by condensation polymerization, where monomers join and a small molecule is eliminated. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Poly(ethene) differs from nylon because poly(ethene) is formed by

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare two polymer types.
explanation.principle: Poly(ethene) comes from ethene, an unsaturated monomer with a C=C bond.
steps: The double bond opens and many monomers join without eliminating any small molecule.
That is addition polymerization.
Nylon, in contrast, is a condensation polymer.
Therefore addition polymerization from unsaturated monomers is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. addition polymerization from unsaturated monomers
explanation.simple_explanation: Poly(ethene) comes from alkene monomers and is an addition polymer. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which of the following is most likely to undergo addition polymerization?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a monomer suitable for addition polymerization.
explanation.principle: Addition polymerization usually requires an unsaturated monomer.
steps: Ethene has a C=C double bond that can open up and link many molecules together.
The other compounds listed do not fit the typical alkene monomer pattern.
So ethene is correct.
This is a core monomer-to-polymer idea.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alkenes such as ethene are classic monomers for addition polymerization because of their double bonds. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Condensation polymerization differs from addition polymerization because condensation polymerization

options.B: usually eliminates a small molecule during chain formation

answer_text: usually eliminates a small molecule during chain formation

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare two polymerization processes.
explanation.principle: In condensation polymerization, reactive functional groups join together.
steps: As the polymer chain forms, a small molecule such as water or HCl is often removed.
This is different from addition polymerization, where no such by-product is usually formed.
Therefore elimination of a small molecule is the key distinction.
This difference is heavily tested in multiple-choice questions.
explanation.final_answer: B. usually eliminates a small molecule during chain formation
explanation.simple_explanation: In condensation polymerization, the monomers join and a small molecule is commonly split out. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Natural and synthetic rubber; thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics
question: Natural rubber is obtained mainly from

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the source of natural rubber.
explanation.principle: Natural rubber comes from latex, a milky fluid obtained from rubber trees.
steps: The latex contains polymeric material that can be processed into rubber.
This is why it is classified as a natural polymer.
Hence latex from rubber trees is correct.
It contrasts with synthetic rubber made industrially.
explanation.simple_explanation: Natural rubber is a plant product obtained from the latex of rubber trees. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Natural and synthetic rubber; thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics
question: A thermoplastic differs from a thermosetting plastic because a thermoplastic

options.B: can be softened repeatedly and remoulded

answer_text: can be softened repeatedly and remoulded

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare thermoplastics with thermosetting plastics.
explanation.principle: Thermoplastics soften when heated and harden again on cooling.
steps: Because this can happen repeatedly, they can be remoulded.
Thermosetting plastics, once set, do not soften easily on reheating because of extensive cross-linking.
Therefore repeated softening and remoulding is the thermoplastic property.
This difference is important in recycling and industrial use.
explanation.final_answer: B. can be softened repeatedly and remoulded
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermoplastics can be reheated and reshaped, unlike thermosetting plastics. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Natural and synthetic rubber; thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics
question: Which statement about thermosetting plastics is correct?

options.A: they soften every time they are heated
options.B: they have extensive cross-links and do not remould easily
options.D: they are monomers rather than polymers

answer_text: they have extensive cross-links and do not remould easily

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe thermosetting plastics.
explanation.principle: Thermosetting plastics form many cross-links between polymer chains during setting.
steps: These cross-links create a rigid network.
Once formed, the material does not melt and flow easily on reheating.
So they do not remould easily.
That is the key property examined here.
explanation.final_answer: B. they have extensive cross-links and do not remould easily
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermosetting plastics become permanently set because their chains are strongly linked together. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Natural and synthetic rubber; thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics
question: Synthetic rubber is often preferred in some applications because it can be

explanation.question_restate: We are to state an advantage of synthetic rubber.
explanation.principle: Synthetic rubber is manufactured under controlled chemical conditions.
steps: Its composition can be adjusted to give properties such as better oil resistance, heat resistance or wear resistance.
This makes it useful for special industrial applications.
Hence it can be designed for desired properties.
That is one major advantage over natural rubber in some cases.
explanation.final_answer: A. designed to possess desired properties
explanation.simple_explanation: Synthetic rubber can be tailored for special uses, which is one reason industries value it. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Natural and synthetic rubber; thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics
question: Which simple diagram best illustrates the difference between a thermoplastic and a thermosetting plastic?

options.A: thermoplastic: heavily cross-linked network; thermoset: separate chains
options.B: thermoplastic: separate or lightly entangled chains; thermoset: strongly cross-linked network
options.D: thermoplastic: ionic lattice; thermoset: metallic lattice

answer_text: thermoplastic: separate or lightly entangled chains; thermoset: strongly cross-linked network

explanation.question_restate: We are to connect structure with heating behaviour.
explanation.principle: Thermoplastics usually have separate chains or only light entanglement, so heat can let the chains slide past one another.
steps: Thermosetting plastics have many cross-links, creating a rigid three-dimensional network.
That is why thermosets do not soften easily on reheating.

A simple sketch is:
Thermoplastic:
~~~~   ~~~~   ~~~~

Thermoset:
~X~X~
 X X
~X~X~
Here X suggests cross-links between chains.
So option B matches the structural idea correctly.
explanation.final_answer: B. thermoplastic: separate or lightly entangled chains; thermoset: strongly cross-linked network
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermoplastics have freer chains, while thermosets are locked together by many cross-links. To build deeper understanding and master more JAMB-style applications, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The tetravalency of carbon means that each carbon atom usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about introduction to tetravalency of carbon in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Carbon has four electrons in its outer shell.
To achieve stability, it shares electrons with other atoms.
This sharing usually allows carbon to form four covalent bonds.
That is why carbon is described as tetravalent.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of carbon as an atom with four 'hands' for bonding. It commonly uses all four to form stable covalent bonds. Check our "Tutorial section" for more beginner-friendly guidance.

question: One major consequence of the tetravalency of carbon is that carbon can

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about introduction to tetravalency of carbon in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Because carbon forms four bonds, one carbon atom can link with other carbon atoms repeatedly.
This ability is called catenation.
It leads to chains, branched chains and ring structures.
That is why organic chemistry contains very many compounds.
explanation.final_answer: A. form long chains and rings
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon can join to itself again and again, making many different structures. This is a major reason organic chemistry is so large. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Carbon forms a very large number of compounds mainly because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about introduction to tetravalency of carbon in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Carbon can form four bonds and also bond strongly with itself.
These two features together produce many stable structures.
The structures may be straight, branched or cyclic.
Hence, a very large number of carbon compounds exist.
explanation.simple_explanation: The key ideas are tetravalency and catenation. Together they explain the huge variety of organic compounds. See our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which statement best explains why methane is stable?

options.B: Carbon shares four pairs of electrons

answer_text: Carbon shares four pairs of electrons

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about introduction to tetravalency of carbon in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: In methane, carbon needs four more electrons to complete its outer shell.
It shares one electron pair with each of four hydrogen atoms.
This gives carbon four covalent bonds.
The arrangement produces a stable molecule, CH₄.
explanation.final_answer: B. Carbon shares four pairs of electrons
explanation.simple_explanation: Methane is stable because carbon shares electrons, not because it transfers them. The sharing creates four single covalent bonds. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The ability of carbon to combine with hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and halogens is largely due to its

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about introduction to tetravalency of carbon in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Carbon can form four covalent bonds with the same or different atoms.
That allows it to combine with hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and halogens in many ways.
The feature responsible is tetravalency.
This is fundamental in organic chemistry.
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon’s four-bond capacity is what makes its chemistry so rich. That property is tetravalency. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The general formula of alkanes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about general formulae in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons containing only single carbon-carbon bonds.
For open-chain alkanes, the hydrogen atoms follow a regular pattern.
That pattern is CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
Examples are CH₄, C₂H₆ and C₃H₈.
explanation.simple_explanation: Remember: alkane = all single bonds = CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for a simple memory guide.

question: A member of the alkene series has the general formula

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about general formulae in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkenes contain one carbon-carbon double bond.
This reduces the number of hydrogen atoms compared with alkanes.
The open-chain alkene general formula is CₙH₂ₙ.
For example, ethene is C₂H₄.
explanation.simple_explanation: The double bond causes two fewer hydrogen atoms than the corresponding alkane. That gives CₙH₂ₙ. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The homologous series with general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about general formulae in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkynes contain a carbon-carbon triple bond.
Because of this higher unsaturation, they have fewer hydrogen atoms than alkenes.
The open-chain general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
An example is ethyne, C₂H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Triple bond means still fewer hydrogens. So alkynes follow CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The general formula of monohydric alkanols is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about general formulae in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: A monohydric alkanol contains one hydroxyl group, –OH.
It may be written in condensed form as CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH.
For example, ethanol is C₂H₅OH.
This corresponds to the alcohol derived from an alkyl group plus hydroxyl.
explanation.simple_explanation: One –OH group attached to an alkyl group gives the pattern CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which formula represents the alkanoic acids?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about general formulae in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkanoic acids contain the carboxyl group, –COOH.
This functional group defines the acid family in organic chemistry.
Their general formula is commonly written as CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH.
Examples include HCOOH and CH₃COOH.
explanation.simple_explanation: The –COOH group is the identifying group for carboxylic acids, so the general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The correct IUPAC name for CH₃CH₂CH₃ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about iupac nomenclature in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The compound has three carbon atoms in a straight chain.
The prefix for three carbons is prop-.
All bonds are single, so the suffix is -ane.
Therefore the name is propane.
explanation.simple_explanation: Count carbons first, then identify the bond type. Three carbons with single bonds give propane. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The IUPAC name of CH₂=CHCH₃ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about iupac nomenclature in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: There are three carbon atoms, so the root is prop-.
There is one C=C double bond, so the suffix is -ene.
Hence the compound is propene.
Its molecular formula is C₃H₆.
explanation.simple_explanation: A double bond changes -ane to -ene. So CH₂=CHCH₃ is propene. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The correct IUPAC name for CH₃CH₂OH is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about iupac nomenclature in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The molecule has two carbon atoms, so the root is eth-.
It contains the hydroxyl group, –OH, of an alcohol.
Alcohols use the suffix -ol.
Therefore the compound is ethanol.
explanation.simple_explanation: Two carbons plus an alcohol group gives ethanol. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: CH₃COOH is correctly named

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about iupac nomenclature in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The compound contains the –COOH group, so it is a carboxylic acid.
There are two carbon atoms in the chain.
The root is ethan-.
Thus the correct name is ethanoic acid.
explanation.simple_explanation: When you see –COOH, think carboxylic acid. Two carbons make it ethanoic acid. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The compound CH₃CHO is named

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about iupac nomenclature in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The molecule contains the aldehyde group, –CHO.
There are two carbon atoms in total.
The root is ethan- and aldehydes take the suffix -al.
Therefore it is ethanal.
explanation.simple_explanation: The –CHO group is the clue. With two carbons, the name is ethanal. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The empirical formula of a compound containing 40% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen and 53.3% oxygen is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about determination of empirical formula in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Assume 100 g of the compound so that masses equal percentages.
Divide by the smallest value, 3.33, to get approximately 1 : 2 : 1.
So the simplest whole-number ratio is CH₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: For empirical formula, convert percentages to moles and simplify to the smallest ratio. That gives CH₂O here. Check our "Tutorial section" for more worked examples.

question: A compound contains carbon and hydrogen only. If 12 g of carbon combine with 3 g of hydrogen, the empirical formula is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about determination of empirical formula in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Find moles of each element.
Carbon: 12/12 = 1 mole. Hydrogen: 3/1 = 3 moles.
The ratio C:H is 1:3.
Hence the empirical formula is CH₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Always change masses to moles before writing empirical formula. Here the simplest ratio is 1:3, so CH₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A compound has molecular formula C₂H₄. Its empirical formula is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about determination of empirical formula in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The molecular formula gives the actual number of atoms in one molecule.
The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio.
For C₂H₄, divide all subscripts by 2.
The simplest ratio becomes CH₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Empirical formula is the simplest ratio, not always the full formula. C₂H₄ simplifies to CH₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: If the mole ratio of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen in a compound is 1 : 2 : 1, the empirical formula is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about determination of empirical formula in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: An empirical formula directly reflects the simplest mole ratio.
The given ratio is C:H:O = 1:2:1.
So write one carbon, two hydrogens and one oxygen.
That gives CH₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: Once the simplest ratio is known, write the symbols in that ratio. So 1:2:1 gives CH₂O. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The first step in determining the empirical formula from percentage composition is to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about determination of empirical formula in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Percentage composition tells you mass in 100 g of sample.
But formulae depend on numbers of atoms, which are compared using moles.
So the first useful step is to convert each percentage to moles.
After that, simplify to the smallest ratio.
explanation.simple_explanation: Masses alone do not give the formula directly. You must first convert them to moles. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which member of the hydrocarbon family is unsaturated?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes/alkenes/alkynes in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain double or triple carbon-carbon bonds.
Butene is an alkene, so it contains a C=C bond.
Ethane, propane and methane are alkanes with only single bonds.
Therefore butene is the unsaturated compound.
explanation.simple_explanation: Unsaturated means not holding the maximum possible hydrogen because of multiple bonds. Butene fits that description. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The simplest alkyne is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes/alkenes/alkynes in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkynes contain a triple bond.
At least two carbon atoms are needed to form a carbon-carbon triple bond.
So the smallest possible alkynes start from two carbons.
Therefore the simplest alkynes is ethyne, C₂H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: You cannot have a carbon-carbon triple bond with only one carbon atom, so the first alkynes is ethyne. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The molecular formula of butane is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes/alkenes/alkynes in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Butane is an alkane.
Alkanes follow the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
For n = 4, the formula becomes C₄H₁₀.
Hence butane is C₄H₁₀.
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the alkane formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. For four carbons, the answer is C₄H₁₀. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which of the following is an alkene?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes/alkenes/alkynes in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Alkenes follow the general formula CₙH₂ₙ for open-chain members.
Among the options, C₄H₈ fits this pattern.
The others fit the alkane formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
So C₄H₈ is the alkene.
explanation.simple_explanation: Match the formula to the series. C₄H₈ fits CₙH₂ₙ, so it is an alkene. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The hydrocarbon with formula C₃H₄ belongs to the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aliphatic hydrocarbons: alkanes/alkenes/alkynes in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Open-chain alkynes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
For n = 3, this gives C₃H₄.
Therefore the compound belongs to the alkyne family.
One example is propyne.
explanation.simple_explanation: Substitute n = 3 into the alkyne formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. You get C₃H₄. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Members of a homologous series differ from one another by a

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about homologous series in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: A homologous series is a family of compounds with the same functional group and similar chemical properties.
Successive members differ by one carbon and two hydrogens.
That difference is a CH₂ unit.
Examples appear clearly in alkanes: CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈.
explanation.simple_explanation: The standard difference between neighbouring members is CH₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: One characteristic feature of members of the same homologous series is that they have

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about homologous series in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Compounds in the same homologous series have the same functional group.
Because of this, they react in similar ways chemically.
Their physical properties, however, often change gradually with size.
So similar chemical properties is the correct statement.
explanation.simple_explanation: The same functional group causes similar reactions. That is the key similarity in a homologous series. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The series CH₃OH, C₂H₅OH, C₃H₇OH belongs to the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about homologous series in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Each member contains the hydroxyl group, –OH.
This identifies the compounds as alcohols or alkanols.
They increase by CH₂ from one member to the next.
Therefore they form the alkanol homologous series.
explanation.simple_explanation: Look for the functional group. The –OH group means alkanols. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Gradual increase in boiling points across a homologous series is mainly due to increase in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about homologous series in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: As members get larger, their molecules become heavier.
Intermolecular forces usually become stronger as molecular size increases.
This causes boiling points to rise gradually along the series.
That is a typical physical trend in homologous series.
explanation.simple_explanation: Bigger molecules usually boil at higher temperatures because the forces between them become stronger. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which statement is true of a homologous series?

options.A: All members have the same empirical formula
options.B: All members have the same functional group
options.C: All members have the same number of carbon atoms
options.D: All members are gases at room temperature

answer_text: All members have the same functional group

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about homologous series in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The defining feature of a homologous series is a common functional group and general formula.
Members differ by CH₂ but keep the same reactive group.
That is why their chemical behavior is related.
Therefore the true statement is that they all have the same functional group.
explanation.final_answer: B. All members have the same functional group
explanation.simple_explanation: Functional group is the chemical 'identity card' of the series. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Structural isomers are compounds with the same

options.C: molecular formula but different structural formulae

answer_text: molecular formula but different structural formulae

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about structural isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Isomers have the same numbers of each type of atom.
However, the atoms are arranged differently.
When the difference is in actual bonding arrangement, they are structural isomers.
Hence they have the same molecular formula but different structures.
explanation.final_answer: C. molecular formula but different structural formulae
explanation.simple_explanation: Same atoms, different arrangement: that is the idea of structural isomerism. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which pair are structural isomers?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about structural isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Both n-butane and 2-methylpropane have the molecular formula C₄H₁₀.
But one is straight-chain while the other is branched.
This difference in carbon skeleton makes them structural isomers.
Therefore that pair is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Same formula, different carbon arrangement: n-butane and 2-methylpropane are classic structural isomers. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The minimum number of carbon atoms required for chain isomerism in alkanes is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about structural isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Methane, ethane and propane have only one possible carbon skeleton each.
But with four carbon atoms, both straight-chain and branched arrangements become possible.
These are butane and 2-methylpropane.
Therefore chain isomerism begins at C₄.
explanation.simple_explanation: The first alkane that can branch is C₄H₁₀. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Compounds that show structural isomerism may differ in their

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about structural isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Structural isomers have the same total numbers of atoms of each element.
So their molecular masses are the same.
What changes is how the atoms are linked together.
That difference in arrangement gives different structural formulae.
explanation.simple_explanation: The 'structure' part of structural isomerism means the bonding arrangement changes. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which hydrocarbon can exist as chain isomers?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about structural isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Chain isomerism needs enough carbon atoms to permit branching.
Pentane, C₅H₁₂, has several possible chain arrangements.
Methane, ethane and propane do not.
So C₅H₁₂ can exist as chain isomers.
explanation.simple_explanation: More carbon atoms allow more possible skeletons. Pentane can branch, so it shows chain isomerism. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Geometric isomerism is commonly found in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about geometric isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: A double bond prevents free rotation of the bonded carbon atoms.
If each carbon of the double bond carries two different groups, different spatial arrangements are possible.
These are geometric isomers such as cis and trans forms.
Therefore alkenes commonly show geometric isomerism.
explanation.simple_explanation: The double bond locks the arrangement, making cis-trans isomerism possible. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Cis-trans isomerism in but-2-ene arises because of the presence of a

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about geometric isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: But-2-ene contains a C=C bond.
This bond restricts rotation and allows the groups around it to remain in fixed relative positions.
So the groups can be on the same side (cis) or opposite sides (trans).
That is why geometric isomerism occurs.
explanation.simple_explanation: The fixed double bond is the reason but-2-ene has cis and trans forms. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which statement about cis-but-2-ene is correct?

options.A: The methyl groups are on opposite sides of the double bond
options.B: The methyl groups are on the same side of the double bond

answer_text: The methyl groups are on the same side of the double bond

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about geometric isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: In cis isomers, similar groups lie on the same side of the double bond.
In but-2-ene, the similar groups are the CH₃ groups.
When they are together on the same side, the molecule is cis-but-2-ene.
If opposite, it is trans-but-2-ene.
explanation.final_answer: B. The methyl groups are on the same side of the double bond
explanation.simple_explanation: Cis means 'same side'. So the CH₃ groups are on the same side in cis-but-2-ene. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A necessary condition for geometric isomerism is that

options.A: the compound must contain a metal
options.C: each carbon of the double bond has two different groups
options.D: the compound must be cyclic only

answer_text: each carbon of the double bond has two different groups

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about geometric isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Not every alkene shows geometric isomerism.
For cis-trans forms to exist, each carbon involved in the double bond must carry two different substituents.
If one carbon has two identical groups, the two arrangements become the same.
Hence that condition is necessary.
explanation.final_answer: C. each carbon of the double bond has two different groups
explanation.simple_explanation: Restricted rotation alone is not enough; the attached groups must also allow two different spatial arrangements. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Trans-but-2-ene differs from cis-but-2-ene in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about geometric isomerism in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Both cis- and trans-but-2-ene have the same molecular and empirical formulae.
They also have the same atoms connected in the same order.
The difference lies in the three-dimensional arrangement around the double bond.
So they differ in spatial arrangement.
explanation.simple_explanation: Same atoms, same bonding order, different positions in space: that is geometric isomerism. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Ethene reacts with bromine water mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about substitution, addition and polymerization reactions in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Ethene contains a carbon-carbon double bond.
Unsaturated compounds often react by addition across the double bond.
Bromine adds to the molecule, causing the bromine water to lose its colour.
Therefore the reaction is addition.
explanation.simple_explanation: A double bond opens up and new atoms add across it. That is addition. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Methane reacts with chlorine in sunlight to form chloromethane by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about substitution, addition and polymerization reactions in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Methane is a saturated hydrocarbon with only single bonds.
In sunlight, one hydrogen atom can be replaced by a chlorine atom.
A replacement reaction is called substitution.
So the process is substitution.
explanation.simple_explanation: Saturated alkanes usually react by replacing one atom with another, not by opening a double bond. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The formation of polyethene from ethene is an example of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about substitution, addition and polymerization reactions in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Ethene molecules contain double bonds.
During polymerization, many ethene molecules join together as the double bonds open.
No small molecule is removed in the process.
Hence the reaction is addition polymerization.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many ethene units add together directly to form a giant chain. That is addition polymerization. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: One test for unsaturation in a hydrocarbon is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about substitution, addition and polymerization reactions in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Unsaturated compounds such as alkenes and alkynes readily add bromine.
This reaction removes the reddish-brown colour of bromine water.
Saturated hydrocarbons generally do not do this under ordinary conditions.
Therefore decolourization of bromine water indicates unsaturation.
explanation.simple_explanation: If bromine water loses its colour, suspect a multiple bond. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which statement correctly compares addition and substitution reactions?

options.A: Addition is typical of alkanes, substitution of alkenes
options.B: Addition reduces saturation while substitution increases it
options.C: Addition is common in unsaturated compounds, substitution in saturated alkanes
options.D: Both occur only in aromatic compounds

answer_text: Addition is common in unsaturated compounds, substitution in saturated alkanes

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about substitution, addition and polymerization reactions in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Unsaturated compounds have double or triple bonds that can open and accept extra atoms.
So they commonly undergo addition.
Saturated alkanes lack multiple bonds and often react by replacing one atom with another.
That replacement is substitution.
explanation.final_answer: C. Addition is common in unsaturated compounds, substitution in saturated alkanes
explanation.simple_explanation: Use this quick rule: multiple bond → addition; alkane → substitution. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Crude oil is best described as a

options.D: compound of carbon and sulfur only

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about petroleum composition/fractional distillation/cracking/reforming/petrochemicals/octane number in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Crude oil, also called petroleum, is not a single substance.
It contains many hydrocarbons with different chain lengths and boiling points.
These components can be separated physically by fractional distillation.
Therefore crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of crude oil as a blend of many hydrocarbon substances, not one pure chemical. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The principle behind fractional distillation of petroleum is difference in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about petroleum composition/fractional distillation/cracking/reforming/petrochemicals/octane number in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The hydrocarbon fractions in crude oil vaporize and condense at different temperatures.
A fractionating column is hotter at the bottom and cooler at the top.
Different components condense at different levels according to their boiling points.
So the separation depends mainly on boiling-point differences.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fractional distillation works because each fraction has its own boiling range. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Cracking is important in the petroleum industry because it

options.A: converts large molecules into smaller useful ones

answer_text: converts large molecules into smaller useful ones

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about petroleum composition/fractional distillation/cracking/reforming/petrochemicals/octane number in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Heavy fractions are less useful and less demanded than lighter fractions such as petrol.
Cracking breaks long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter hydrocarbons.
These products often include alkanes and alkenes.
Therefore cracking increases the supply of more useful smaller molecules.
explanation.final_answer: A. converts large molecules into smaller useful ones
explanation.simple_explanation: Cracking is like cutting long hydrocarbon chains into shorter, more valuable ones. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A high octane number indicates that a fuel

options.A: ignites too easily and knocks badly

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about petroleum composition/fractional distillation/cracking/reforming/petrochemicals/octane number in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Octane number measures how well petrol resists premature ignition.
A fuel with a higher octane number burns more smoothly in spark-ignition engines.
This reduces knocking or engine pinging.
Hence high octane number means greater resistance to knocking.
explanation.final_answer: C. resists knocking in an engine
explanation.simple_explanation: High octane fuel gives smoother engine performance because it resists self-ignition. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Ethene and propene used in making plastics are examples of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about petroleum composition/fractional distillation/cracking/reforming/petrochemicals/octane number in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Petrochemicals are chemical substances obtained from petroleum or natural gas.
Simple alkenes such as ethene and propene are major petrochemical feedstocks.
They are used to make polymers, solvents and many industrial chemicals.
Therefore they are examples of petrochemicals.
explanation.simple_explanation: Petrochemicals are useful chemicals made from petroleum fractions. Ethene and propene are classic examples. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Vulcanization improves natural rubber mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about vulcanization and rubber in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Natural rubber contains long polymer chains that can move too freely.
During vulcanization, sulfur forms cross-links between the chains.
This makes the rubber stronger, less sticky and more elastic over a wider temperature range.
Therefore sulfur cross-linking is the key change.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sulfur ties the rubber chains together, improving strength and durability. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: One effect of vulcanization on rubber is that it becomes

options.B: more durable and less sensitive to temperature

answer_text: more durable and less sensitive to temperature

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about vulcanization and rubber in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Untreated rubber softens too much when hot and becomes brittle when cold.
Vulcanization reduces these weaknesses.
The product is tougher, more elastic and more resistant to wear.
So it becomes more durable and less temperature-sensitive.
explanation.final_answer: B. more durable and less sensitive to temperature
explanation.simple_explanation: Vulcanization makes rubber more useful in real life because it behaves better in heat and cold. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Natural rubber is classified as a

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about vulcanization and rubber in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Natural rubber consists of many repeating units joined into very large molecules.
Such giant molecules are called polymers.
Its monomer is related to isoprene.
Therefore natural rubber is a polymer.
explanation.simple_explanation: A polymer is a giant molecule built from many small repeating units. Natural rubber fits this description. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Which substance is commonly used in vulcanizing rubber?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about vulcanization and rubber in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The standard vulcanizing agent for rubber is sulfur.
When heated with rubber, sulfur forms bridges between polymer chains.
These links improve mechanical properties.
Thus sulfur is the correct substance.
explanation.simple_explanation: For JAMB purposes, remember vulcanization = heating rubber with sulfur. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Synthetic rubber differs from natural rubber mainly because synthetic rubber is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about vulcanization and rubber in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Synthetic rubbers are manufactured chemically, usually from petrochemical monomers.
Natural rubber is obtained from latex of rubber plants.
Both are polymers, but their sources differ.
Hence synthetic rubber is typically made from petrochemicals.
explanation.simple_explanation: Natural rubber comes from plants; synthetic rubber is industrially made from petroleum-derived chemicals. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Benzene is classified as an aromatic compound because it

options.B: contains a stable ring of delocalized electrons

answer_text: contains a stable ring of delocalized electrons

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Aromaticity is not simply about smell.
Benzene has a six-carbon ring with a special stable electron arrangement.
The π electrons are delocalized around the ring.
This delocalization gives unusual stability and aromatic character.
explanation.final_answer: B. contains a stable ring of delocalized electrons
explanation.simple_explanation: Benzene is aromatic because of its special stable ring-electron system, not because of odour. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The molecular formula of benzene is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Benzene contains six carbon atoms arranged in a ring.
It also contains six hydrogen atoms, one attached to each carbon.
So its molecular formula is C₆H₆.
This is a classic formula to memorize.
explanation.simple_explanation: Benzene is the famous six-carbon aromatic ring: C₆H₆. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Benzene tends to undergo substitution rather than addition because

options.B: addition would destroy its aromatic stability

answer_text: addition would destroy its aromatic stability

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The delocalized ring of benzene is unusually stable.
An addition reaction would break this special electron arrangement.
So benzene usually reacts by substitution, preserving the ring system.
That is why aromatic compounds often behave differently from alkenes.
explanation.final_answer: B. addition would destroy its aromatic stability
explanation.simple_explanation: Benzene 'protects' its stable aromatic ring, so substitution is preferred. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A common use of benzene derivatives is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Many aromatic compounds derived from benzene are useful industrial raw materials.
They are used in making dyes, plastics, detergents, drugs and explosives.
So dyes and plastics is a correct general application.
Benzene chemistry is important in industry.
explanation.simple_explanation: Benzene derivatives are building blocks for many manufactured products, especially dyes and plastics. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: In older representations, the structure of benzene is often shown as a hexagon with

options.A: a triple bond outside the ring
options.B: a chlorine atom at the centre
options.C: alternating double bonds or a circle

answer_text: alternating double bonds or a circle

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: The traditional Kekulé structure shows alternating single and double bonds.
Modern representation often uses a circle inside the hexagon.
Both drawings try to show the delocalized electrons in the ring.
Therefore the accepted description is alternating double bonds or a circle.
explanation.final_answer: C. alternating double bonds or a circle
explanation.simple_explanation: If you see a hexagon with a circle or alternating double bonds, think benzene. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Ethanol belongs to the class of compounds known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about alkanols and lucas test in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Ethanol has the functional group –OH.
Organic compounds with this group attached to an alkyl chain are called alcohols or alkanols.
Therefore ethanol is an alkanol.
Its formula is C₂H₅OH.
explanation.simple_explanation: The hydroxyl group identifies an alkanol. Ethanol is a simple example. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A primary alkanol is one in which the carbon carrying the –OH group is attached to

options.C: one other carbon atom or none in methanol

answer_text: one other carbon atom or none in methanol

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about alkanols and lucas test in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Classification of alcohols depends on the carbon atom bearing the hydroxyl group.
If that carbon is attached to only one other carbon atom, the alcohol is primary.
Methanol is also treated as primary because the carbon is attached to no other carbon.
So option C is correct.
explanation.final_answer: C. one other carbon atom or none in methanol
explanation.simple_explanation: Look only at the carbon holding the –OH group. If it is linked to at most one other carbon, the alcohol is primary. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The Lucas test is used mainly to distinguish

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about alkanols and lucas test in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: Lucas reagent reacts with alcohols at different speeds depending on their class.
Tertiary alcohols react fastest, secondary react more slowly, while primary alcohols react very slowly at room temperature.
This helps in distinguishing primary, secondary and tertiary alkanols.
Hence option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. primary, secondary and tertiary alkanols
explanation.simple_explanation: Lucas test classifies alcohols by how quickly cloudiness appears. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: A tertiary alkanol gives immediate turbidity with Lucas reagent because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about alkanols and lucas test in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: In the Lucas test, substitution occurs more readily when a stable carbocation can form.
Tertiary alcohols form the most stable carbocations among the three classes.
Therefore they react fastest and show immediate cloudiness.
That is why turbidity appears quickly.
explanation.final_answer: A. forms a carbocation most easily
explanation.simple_explanation: Tertiary alcohols react fastest in the Lucas test because their intermediate is most stable. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Glycerol is described as a trihydric alcohol because it contains

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct idea about alkanols and lucas test in this organic chemistry question.
explanation.principle: Organic chemistry questions in JAMB often test recognition of functional groups, class properties, reaction patterns, formula relationships, and simple laboratory applications.
steps: A hydric number tells how many –OH groups are present.
Glycerol has three hydroxyl groups in one molecule.
That makes it a trihydric alcohol.
Its systematic name is propane-1,2,3-triol.
explanation.simple_explanation: Trihydric means 'three hydroxyl groups'. Glycerol has three of them. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

subtopic: Ethanol by fermentation and petroleum by-products
question: Ethanol can be prepared by fermentation of

steps: Aldehydes are more easily oxidized than ketones.
Tollens' reagent oxidizes aldehydes and gives a silver mirror.
Ketones generally do not react under the same conditions.
So it is used to distinguish alkanals from alkanones.
explanation.simple_explanation: Tollens' reagent is the classic aldehyde test because of the silver mirror. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

Therefore a brick-red precipitate indicates an alkanal.
explanation.simple_explanation: Blue Fehling's turning brick-red is a strong clue for an aldehyde. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The functional group of an alkanone is

subtopic: Alkanoic acids: neutralization, esterification, ethanedioic acid, benzenecarboxylic acid

subtopic: Alkanoates: fats and oils, saponification, soap/margarine, detergents vs soaps

steps: A secondary amine has two alkyl groups attached to nitrogen and one hydrogen remaining.
(CH₃)₂NH fits that description.
CH₃NH₂ is primary and (CH₃)₃N is tertiary.
Therefore the secondary amine is (CH₃)₂NH.

All three hydrogens of NH₃ are replaced by alkyl groups.
So the nitrogen is attached to three carbon-containing groups.
Hence option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Primary = one alkyl; secondary = two; tertiary = three. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: Enzymes are important because they

question: Why is carbon able to form long chains and rings more readily than silicon in ordinary organic chemistry?

options.A: Carbon atoms readily form strong C–C covalent bonds
options.B: Carbon has only one valence electron
options.C: Carbon forms ionic bonds with itself

answer_text: Carbon atoms readily form strong C–C covalent bonds

explanation.question_restate: We need the main reason carbon builds long chains and rings so successfully.
explanation.principle: Carbon shows catenation because carbon–carbon covalent bonds are strong and stable.
steps: Each carbon atom can share electrons with other carbon atoms.
The C–C bond is strong enough to remain stable in chains and ring systems.
Because of this, carbon forms straight chains, branched chains and cyclic compounds.
That is why organic chemistry contains an enormous variety of compounds.
explanation.final_answer: A. Carbon atoms readily form strong C–C covalent bonds

question: A carbon atom in ethane, C₂H₆, is described as saturated because it

explanation.principle: A saturated organic compound contains only single bonds between carbon atoms.
steps: In ethane, each carbon uses four bonds in total.
The two carbon atoms are joined by one single bond, and the remaining bonds go to hydrogen.
There is no C=C or C≡C bond present.
So the compound is saturated, meaning it cannot take in more hydrogen by simple addition across a multiple bond.
explanation.final_answer: B. is bonded only by single covalent bonds

steps: Methane has the formula CH₄.

That gives the formula C₂H₆.

explanation.simple_explanation: This idea is easier when you connect the name, formula, functional group and reaction pattern step by step. For fuller beginner-friendly teaching and more worked examples, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The huge number of carbon compounds is mainly due to the combination of tetravalency and

steps: With four bonds, carbon can join many atoms around it.
With catenation, carbon can form chains, branches and rings with itself.
When both properties act together, the number of possible structures becomes very large.
Hence the variety of carbon compounds is mainly due to tetravalency plus catenation.

question: A compound contains 75% carbon and 25% hydrogen by mass. Its empirical formula is

explanation.principle: To find an empirical formula, convert masses to moles and then reduce to the smallest whole-number ratio.
steps: Assume 100 g of the compound. Then carbon = 75 g and hydrogen = 25 g.
Moles of carbon = 75/12 = 6.25, while moles of hydrogen = 25/1 = 25.
Divide both values by 6.25 to obtain the ratio 1:4.
So the empirical formula is CH₄.

steps: Divide by the smallest, about 3.33, to get C:H:O ≈ 1:2:1.

Hence the empirical formula is CH₂O.

Divide both by 7.14 to get approximately 1:2.

steps: The empirical formula mass of CH₂ is 12 + 2 = 14.

explanation.principle: A homologous series contains compounds with the same functional group and general formula.
steps: The members have similar chemical properties because they possess the same functional group.
When you move from one member to the next, one extra –CH₂– unit is added.
Their physical properties change gradually rather than remaining identical.

question: Which hydrocarbon exhibits geometric isomerism?

steps: Ethene does not show cis–trans isomerism because each carbon has two identical hydrogens.
But-2-ene has a C=C bond and each double-bonded carbon is attached to two different groups.
This allows cis-but-2-ene and trans-but-2-ene to exist.

As bromine is used up, the reddish-brown colour disappears.

question: Which representation best describes the bonding in benzene?

explanation.principle: The π electrons in benzene are delocalized over the ring.
steps: Older drawings show alternating double bonds, but the actual bonding is more uniform than that picture suggests.
All six C–C bonds in benzene are of equal length because the π electrons are spread around the ring.
This special stability explains why benzene does not behave exactly like a normal alkene.
So the best description is a six-membered ring with delocalized π electrons.
explanation.final_answer: B. A six-membered ring with delocalized π electrons

question: In the Lucas test, a tertiary alcohol gives turbidity

options.A: very slowly or not at all at room temperature
options.B: only after heating strongly for several hours
options.D: only in the presence of bromine water

explanation.question_restate: We are recalling the Lucas test behavior of alcohol classes.
explanation.principle: Lucas reagent helps distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols by the speed of cloudiness formation.
steps: Tertiary alcohols form alkyl chlorides most readily in the reagent.
Because the product is insoluble, the mixture becomes cloudy quickly.
Secondary alcohols react more slowly, while primary alcohols are slow or show little change at room temperature.
Therefore a tertiary alcohol gives turbidity almost immediately.
explanation.final_answer: C. almost immediately at room temperature

This difference helps distinguish the two classes.

question: Fats and oils are naturally occurring esters of glycerol and

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall what combines with glycerol to form fats and oils.

steps: Glycerol is a triol with three –OH groups.

explanation.question_restate: We are defining saponification.
explanation.principle: Saponification is an important reaction of esters in fats and oils.
steps: When fats or oils are heated with alkali such as NaOH, the ester bonds are broken.
Glycerol is produced together with the sodium or potassium salts of fatty acids.
These salts are soaps.
Hence saponification is the alkaline hydrolysis of fats and oils to give soap.

question: Nylon is commonly classified as a

explanation.question_restate: We are classifying nylon.
explanation.principle: Nylon is formed from monomers that join with elimination of small molecules.
steps: This places it in the condensation polymer class.
Nylon is a synthetic fibre with strong and useful mechanical properties.
It is not an addition polymer like poly(ethene).
Hence nylon is a condensation polymer.
explanation.simple_explanation: This idea is easier when you connect the name, formula, functional group and reaction pattern step by step. For fuller beginner-friendly teaching and more worked examples, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The repeating unit in poly(ethene) is

explanation.question_restate: We are identifying the repeating unit produced when ethene polymerizes.
explanation.principle: The repeating unit comes from the monomer after the double bond opens.
steps: Ethene is CH₂=CH₂.
When many molecules join, the double bond disappears and each unit becomes –CH₂–CH₂– in the chain.
Text sketch: nCH₂=CH₂ → (–CH₂–CH₂–)ₙ
Therefore the repeating unit is –CH₂–CH₂–.
explanation.simple_explanation: This idea is easier when you connect the name, formula, functional group and reaction pattern step by step. For fuller beginner-friendly teaching and more worked examples, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Natural rubber is obtained mainly from

explanation.question_restate: We are identifying the source of natural rubber.
explanation.principle: Natural rubber is a plant product obtained from latex.
steps: Latex is a milky fluid collected from rubber trees.
It contains rubber particles that can be processed into usable material.
This makes natural rubber a natural polymer.
